name: Joey Cantillo
reasoning: No injury or health concerns surfaced for Cantillo — he's on his normal turn (confirmed starter for 7/24 at Tampa Bay per MLB.com probables) after five days rest, which matches the stats-only model's assumption. Recent form strongly supports the bullish trailing K/9 numbers: beat coverage notes he enters/exits the break 'in excellent form, posting a 1.59 ERA over his last six starts, including three outings with nine strikeouts,' and he leaned heavily on a plus curveball (a season-high 44 curveballs in one outing, with opponents going 2-for-22 against it), suggesting swing-and-miss stuff is trending up, not down. One mild yellow flag from a same-day betting preview is that he 'has walked more hitters per nine than McClanahan and hasn't had quite the same command night to night,' which could theoretically shorten some outings, but his last outing before this stretch was actually a season-high 8-inning start, indicating the staff is stretching him out rather than capping his workload. The Rays lineup is deep and hot (Junior Caminero slugging .559 with 29 HR, Victor Mesa Jr. red-hot), which raises scoring/contact risk somewhat, but no specific data emerged showing this lineup is unusually whiff-prone or K-vulnerable against left-handers, so it doesn't materially contradict the model. Overall the qualitative findings corroborate the stats-only model's optimism, so I'm landing just below its 6.3 projection to account for the modest walk/command variance risk against a good-hitting Rays club, well above the 4.5 market line.

name: Roki Sasaki
reasoning: Sasaki's last two starts (6 IP/5K vs. Rockies, 5.2 IP/5K vs. Yankees) both landed right at 5 strikeouts, and his fastball has spiked to a career-best 100.1 mph with peaks over 101 mph, which is more bat-missing stuff than the model's inputs may fully capture. He's also shown better command/strikes recently (94 pitches, 58 strikes vs. NYY) even though season-long he's been homer-prone and volatile (4.98 ERA, 34 BB). The Mets are a last-place (43-60) offense that the Dodgers outscored 14-3 in their last meeting, supporting a favorable matchup, so I'm nudging slightly above the stats-only model's 4.1 projection toward the market line rather than below it, given the tangible velocity/swing-and-miss uptick in his last two outings.

name: Tomoyuki Sugano
reasoning: Sugano's most recent start (July 18 vs. Reds, his first back from a back-spasm IL stint) produced just 3 strikeouts over 6.1 innings with zero walks, reinforcing his season-long profile as a pitch-to-contact arm who 'won't pile up strikeouts, but can pitch to contact and eat innings' rather than miss bats. His season total of 51 K's in roughly 90 innings works out to a rate consistent with the model's 4.0 trailing K/9, and there's no reported velocity spike, role change, or workload cap tonight — he's fully healthy and lined up for his regular turn. Milwaukee's lineup is a solid contact team (.253 team average, 4th in NL) rather than a swing-and-miss group, which slightly reinforces rather than boosts the model's modest strikeout expectation, so I land just under the model's 3.8 and roughly in line with the 3.5 market number.
